segments.s292-g81: به زیبد ببد is an archaic/poetic impersonal phrase meaning 'it was fitting' or 'it pleased (one)'; here it governs the clause. شاه is the logical subject/beneficiary ('the king') that follows the predicate (inverted word order typical in poetry). یک هفته specifies a duration 'for one week' and نیز ('also') adds the sense that this giving was in addition to other gifts or actions.
segments.s292-g82: درم داد is the main verbal action: درم (dirham coin) + داد (past of دادن, 'gave'). The following و connects additional items: دینار ('dinar, money') and then a generalizing phrase هر گونه چیز ('every kind of thing, anything'), where هر گونه modifies چیز to mean 'all sorts of things.'
segments.s292-g83: فرستاد is the past verb 'sent' (third person). هر سو is a compound meaning 'in every direction' (هر = 'every', سو = 'side/direction'). فرستادگان is the plural 'messengers' or 'envoys' and is the object of فرستاد.
segments.s292-g84: به نزد is a dative/locative prepositional phrase meaning 'to (the presence of)' or 'before.' بزرگان ('the great ones') is the first addressee and آزادگان ('the free' i.e., nobles, freemen) is the second, joined by و ('and'). The whole phrase gives the recipients/places the envoys were sent to.
segments.s292-g85: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' از جنگ پیران literally 'from the war of the elders' (از = 'from/of', جنگ = 'war', پیران = 'elders'); شدی is second-person past/perfect 'you became' or 'you are (become)'; بی‌نیاز is a predicate adjective 'free, without need' — together meaning 'when you have become free from the elders' war.'
segments.s292-g86: یکی here means 'one' or 'a single'; رزم is 'battle' or 'single combat'; کیخسرو is the beneficiary/name 'Kay Khosrow'; اکنون 'now' is a temporal adverb; بساز is an imperative/optative form of ساختن ('make/arrange/prepare'), instructing the addressee to 'prepare/arrange' that one battle for Kay Khosrow.
segments.s292-g1: از آن پس is a temporal phrase meaning 'after that' (از = from; آن پس = then/afterwards). خروش is the subject 'a cry' and آمد is the past‑tense verb 'came/rose' (3rd singular). از دیده‌گاه is a prepositional complement 'from the lookout/field of sight' specifying the source of the cry; دیده‌گاه is a poetic compound meaning 'lookout/viewpoint'.
segments.s292-g2: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause 'for/that'. گرد سواران is a noun phrase meaning 'a band/host of horsemen' (گرد here has a distributive/collective sense 'around/round' → 'a band'). برآمد is the simple past 'rose/appeared/emerged'. ز راه is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the road' (ز = poetic variant of از).
segments.s292-g3: سه اسب و دو کشته lists the visible items 'three horses and two slain (bodies)'. بر او بسته is a participial/adjectival phrase 'tied/bound on him' (بر = upon, او = him; بسته is the past participle/adjective 'bound'). زار is an adjectival remark 'pitiful/wretched', qualifying the scene or the bound state.
segments.s292-g4: همی is an archaic/prose‑poetic particle marking continuing or simple present action (here 'I see'); بینم is the 1st‑person present of دیدن 'I see'. از دور is the adverbial 'from afar' and با یک سوار 'with one rider' indicates accompaniment or that the sight includes a single rider. Word order places the verbal phrase 'همی بینم' before the locative and accompanying phrases.
segments.s292-g5: همه نامداران ایران is a noun phrase 'all the renowned men of Iran' (همه = all; نامداران = famed persons; ایران = of Iran). سپاه is the direct object of the following verb (سپاه نهادند = 'set/raised an army' or 'mustered a host'). The phrase is compact: the agent phrase precedes the object 'سپاه'.
segments.s292-g6: نهادند is the 3rd‑person plural past of نهادن 'they set/placed' (here 'they marshalled' or 'they fixed'). چشم is the object 'their eyes'; از شگفتی is a phrase 'from astonishment' (از marks cause/starting point) meaning 'in wonder/astonished'; به راه is a directional phrase 'towards the road'—together: 'they set their eyes, astonished, on the road.'
segments.s292-g7: که here begins an indirect/rhetorical question 'to see who...'. تا is an emphatic/limitative particle often used in questions ('just who/indeed who'); کیست is the fused interrogative/copular expression 'who is it'. از مرز توران زمین is a prepositional phrase 'from the border of Turan's land' identifying origin—word order: the origin phrase follows the interrogative.
segments.s292-g8: که again links the relative clause 'who' (i.e., 'who is he who...'). یارد is 'ally/companion/helper' used here to denote a would‑be entrant or aid (poetic usage). گذشتن is the infinitive 'to cross/pass', governed implicitly by the relative/question; بر این دشت کین = 'over this plain of vengeance' (بر = over/on, این = this, دشت = plain, کین = vengeance/hatred). The clause overall expresses incredulity about who from Turan would cross this hostile plain.
segments.s292-g17: ببوسید is a 3rd-person past form meaning 'he kissed' (classical past tense). و is the coordinating 'and'. بر خاک بنهاد روی is a compact classical phrase: بنهاد (from نهادن) is a past 3rd-person 'he placed/laid', بر خاک = 'upon the ground', and روی here means 'face' — together 'he laid his face upon the ground.' Word order is verb + object/locative typical of older Persian narrative.
segments.s292-g18: بشد is the classical past/auxiliary sense 'it became' (here 'he became'); شاد is predicate adjective 'happy'. خسرو is the subject; به دیدار اوی is a prepositional phrase 'at the sight of him / on seeing him' — اوی is an archaic/pronominal form referring to 'him'. The clause order (verb — predicate — subject — prepositional phrase) reflects older poetic syntax.
segments.s292-g19: بپرسید is the 3rd-person past 'he asked'. و links clauses. گفتش is 'he said to him' (گفت + enclitic ش = 'to him'). که‌ای here begins the reported speech: classical sequence where که + ای (vocative) introduces direct address — 'O...'; شیر مرد is a compound vocative 'lion / man' meaning 'brave man' or 'hero'.
segments.s292-g20: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. رفته is the past participle/verb 'gone'. بودی is the past form of بودن used here as auxiliary to form the pluperfect/compound past ('had you gone' / 'you had gone'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from', دشت 'plain/field', نبرد 'battle' — together 'from the field of battle.' Word order places the auxiliary after the participle as in classical Persian.
segments.s292-g21: ز here is the ablative/causal 'from / because of'. گستهم is the poetical first-person past form ('I wandered') used nominally = 'my wandering'. بیژن is the subject; سخن یاد کرد is a common classical collocation 'remembered/recalled the matter' (سخن = 'talk/matter', یاد کرد = 'made remembrance' = 'recalled' or 'spoke of it').
segments.s292-g22: This short clause lists causes introduced by ز/و (poetic connectors). ز لهاک = 'from/for the eagerness/longing' (لهاک = longing/eagerness). وز here is a fused form (و + ز) typical in poetry meaning 'and from' (not a lexical 'gust' sense), followed by گرد فرشیدورد where گرد = 'dust' (the dust raised) and فرشیدورد is a proper name. The whole: 'from eagerness and from the dust (raised by) Farshidvard.'
segments.s292-g23: وز آن here again shows the poetic fused particle (و + ز / از) introducing causes; آن = 'that'. خسته 'tired/weariness' and زاری 'lamentation' describe the speaker's state; گستهم again is the 1st-person past used nominally ('my wandering'). The clause lists the speaker's fatigue and lamentation as reasons/circumstances.
segments.s292-g24: ز (poetic 'from') جنگ سواران = 'from the war/fighting of the horsemen' (سواران = mounted fighters). و connects, از بیش و کم = 'from the more and the less' (i.e., from the many and the few / from excess and lack) — a compact poetic way to indicate 'from all kinds / from abundance and scarcity.'
segments.s292-g33: چنان + adjective (بد) is a fixed classical pattern meaning 'so/that ...' (intensifier + state). ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' introducing the cause; بس is an intensifier here meaning 'much/very' and خستگی is the noun 'fatigue/weariness'. گَستهم is an archaic/poetic past-form (1st/3rd-person appearance in classical usage) marking the resulting state ('was / became exhausted'); it closes the clause and links cause to result.
segments.s292-g34: که introduces the result clause ('so that/that'); گفتی here is used idiomatically ('one might say' / 'it may be said') rather than its literal senses in the lexicon; همی is the classical progressive/emphatic particle (common in Ferdowsi) placed before the verb; برنیامدش is composed of the preverb بر- + نیامد (past of آمدن) + enclitic -ش (him/her) meaning 'did not come forth / failed to come out' (i.e. no breath issued); دم is 'breath' (literal).
segments.s292-g35: یکی here is an indefinite 'someone' or 'one (of them)'; بوی مهر شهنشاه is a genitive/attributive group 'the scent (breeze) of the king's favour/affection' with noun-before-verb word order typical of classical Persian; یافت is the simple past 'found/caught'. The verb comes after its object in the usual Persian SOV pattern.
segments.s292-g36: بپیچید is the past form with the prefix بَـ (classical preverb) meaning 'turned (around/aside)'; و = 'and' connects the two coordinated actions; دیده is the subject 'the eye' (i.e. gaze); سوی او is a prepositional phrase 'toward him' (سوی = toward, او = him); شتافت is past 'hastened' — sequence: he turned and then his gaze rushed toward the king.
segments.s292-g37: ببارید is a 3rd-person past singular verb meaning literally 'it rained / fell in drops' and here figuratively describes tears falling; از دیدگان is the ablative 'from the eyes'; آب مهر is a poetic phrase 'the water of love' = 'tears of affection' where آب is the substantive and مهر qualifies it.
segments.s292-g38: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed' (a high-ranking officer); پر از ... چهر is a verbless descriptive cluster 'face full of water and blood' where پر از = 'full of' (prepositional/adjectival), آب و خون are the contents, and کرد is the causative/past verb 'made' (literally 'made the face full'); چهر ('face') is the object affected. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect (modifier before verb).
segments.s292-g39: بزرگان is the plural subject 'the great ones/elders'; بر او is a prepositional phrase 'over/about him' (showing the object of their grief); زار و گریان شدند is a compound predicate: زار (here 'lamenting, wretched') and گریان شدند 'became weeping' — شدند is the past plural of شدن 'to become'. The coordination zarf/adj + participle yields 'became sorrowful and wept.'
segments.s292-g40: چو is a conjunction 'as/when/like' introducing the simile; بر آتش تیز literally 'on a sharp/fire fierce' (بر = 'on/upon', آتش = 'fire', تیز = 'sharp/fierce') — the phrase describes intense torment; بریان شدند is the past passive-like phrase 'were roasted/consumed' used metaphorically to convey the intensity of their grief. The clause compares their weeping to being seared by a hot fire.
segments.s292-g25: کنون is an adverbial fronting meaning “now.” آرزو is the noun “wish/desire.” گستهم is a proper name (not supplied in the senses list), and را is the classical definite/object marker attached after the possessor phrase; together آرزو گستهم را = “Gostaham’s desire.” یکیست is a copular phrase “is one / is a single thing,” completing the predication.
segments.s292-g26: که introduces a subordinate clause (“that”). آن کار is the demonstrative + noun “that task/affair” and is the clause subject. بر شاه دشوار نیست is a predicate with دشوار (adjective “difficult”) and the negative copula نیست; بر marks the experiencer/beneficiary (“for the king”). The clause therefore means “that that task is not difficult for the king.”
segments.s292-g27: به دیدار شاه is a prepositional phrase “to/for a meeting with the king” (i.e. to see the king). آمدستش is a poetic compound of آمد + enclitic pronoun (ـش) meaning “came upon him / befell him.” هوا is the abstract noun “desire/longing.” Word order is English-inverted: literal “to the king’s meeting came upon him desire” → “a desire to see the king came upon him.”
segments.s292-g28: وز آن پس is an archaising sequence meaning roughly “and from that time after” (here treated as a temporal link); اگر introduces a conditional (“if”). میرد is the present-form used in a conditional sense (“if he dies / should die”); او را uses the object/topic marker after the pronoun; روا is a predicate adjective “fitting / permissible.” The whole means “and after that, if he dies, it would be fitting.”
segments.s292-g29: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past verb meaning “he ordered/said.” پس is a sequential adverb “then.” شاه is the subject “the king.” آزرم is a proper name (the addressee/target of the order). جوی is an imperative form (from جستن, “to seek”) meaning “seek!” — classical word order places the object (آزرم) before the imperative verb.
segments.s292-g30: که introduces the reported/result clause “that …”. بردند is a 3rd‑person plural past (“they took/carried/brought”). گستهم is the personal name Gostaham (not represented by the supplied sense), and را is the definite object marker. پیش اوی is a prepositional phrase “before him / in his presence” with the archaic pronoun اوی (“him”). Together: “that they brought Gostaham before him.”
segments.s292-g31: چنان is an intensifier “so” / “thus.” نیک‌دل is the predicate adjective “kind‑hearted, well‑disposed.” شد is the past form of شدن “became.” از او here expresses the relation/cause/target (“toward him / because of him”), and شهریار (placed after the phrase) is the subject “the king.” The clause thus reads “the king became so kind‑hearted because/toward him.”
segments.s292-g32: که introduces the result/explanatory clause “that …”. از گریه مژگانش literally “from the weeping of his eyelashes” (a poetic image: his tears); آمد به بار is an idiomatic sequence (آمد + به بار) meaning “came to bear fruit / took effect / was realized.” So the clause means “that, because of the weeping of his eyelashes, it bore fruit (i.e. produced the desired effect).”
segments.s292-g9: هم اندر زمان — a temporal/adverbial frame: هم 'also/at the same time' precedes اندر زمان 'in the time/at the moment'. بیژن آمد — standard Subject‑Verb order: Bijan (subject) + آمد (past tense 'came'). دمان (damān) is a poetic noun 'hem/skirt' placed after آمد to describe Bijan's appearance as he arrived (i.e. his robe's hem trailing); it functions as a descriptive complement.
segments.s292-g10: به بازو به زه — two instrumental prepositional phrases marked by به (here 'by/with'), listing the instruments or means (with his arm, with the bow‑string). بر فگنده کمان — فگنده is a past participle 'having cast/loosed/thrown' that attaches to the implied subject (Bijan); بر introduces the target/position ('on/against') and کمان is the direct object 'bow'. Together this phrase qualifies how he came (he had loosed/thrown the bow using his arm and the string).
segments.s292-g11: بر اسبان — a locative phrase 'upon/on the horses' with بر. چو introduces a simile 'like/as', linking what follows to the manner of the action. Here چو modifies the following nouns; فرشیدورد is a proper name. The overall phrase means 'on the horses, like Lahāk and Farshidvard'.
segments.s292-g12: فگنده is the same past participle 'having thrown/placed' carried over to describe the subsequent result; نگونسار is an adjective 'woeful/mournful' describing those affected; پر here is the adjectival construction 'full (of)' (پر + از + N is common), taking خون و گرد 'blood and dust' as its complement. Grammatically the participle and adjective form a verbless clause describing the state of those cast down: 'thrown/laid, woeful, full of blood and dust.'
segments.s292-g13: بر اسبی دگر — 'on another horse' (بر + اسبی + دگر; دگر modifies اسبی = 'another/other horse'). بر پر از درد و غم — the second بر marks location 'on (its) back', with پر از 'full of' followed by علت/contents 'درد و غم' (pain and sorrow); together: 'on another horse, on (its) back full of pain and sorrow.'
segments.s292-g14: به آغوش ترک اندرون — a motion phrase 'into the embrace of the Turk, inside' with به indicating motion toward/into, آغوش 'embrace/bosom', ترک 'a Turk' (object of that embrace), and اندرون 'within/inner part' specifying location. گستهم is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'I/he went/entered/wandered'; here it functions as the narrative past verb 'entered/penetrated' (the subject is the previously mentioned actor).
segments.s292-g15: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بیژن is the subject; به نزدیک خسرو is a prepositional phrase 'near/close to Khosrow' (به + نزدیک + خسرو) indicating proximity. رسید is the simple past 'arrived/reached' and completes the temporal clause.
segments.s292-g16: سر تاج و تخت — a noun cluster 'the (head/high) crown and throne' where سر (here 'top/summit' or figuratively 'the head adorned') introduces تاج و تخت 'crown and throne'. بلندش is بلند + enclitic ش 'his/its', meaning 'his lofty/high', and بدید is the past 'saw' (3rd person). Grammatically this is a simple NP + predicate: 'he saw his lofty crown and throne.'
segments.s292-g41: دریغ آمد is an idiom: literally 'pity came' and functions as 'felt regret' or 'was grieved'. او را marks the experiencer (to him / he felt it) and سپهبد names that experiencer ('the spahbed'). به مرگ is a prepositional phrase giving the cause/occasion ('at the death'). Word order is emphatic/poetic: verb-phrase (دریغ آمد) precedes the experiencer marked with را.
segments.s292-g42: که introduces a causal subordinate clause ('for / because'). سندان کین is a genitive compound 'the anvil of (bitter) vengeance' where کین is qualified by بد ('bitter/evil'). سرش is 'his head' (سر + enclitic ش). زیر ترگ is a prepositional phrase 'under a rag'; the verb is omitted for poetic concision, so the clause implies 'was placed/was crushed under'.
segments.s292-g43: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' indicating source; the three proper names are coordinated with و ('and'). The whole phrase lists originators or antecedents ('from Hushang, Tahmuras, and Jamshid').
segments.s292-g44: یکی مهره بد is a noun phrase 'a certain bead/amulet (ill-omened)'; یکی is an indefinite 'one/a certain', مهره is the head noun and بد qualifies it ('bad/evil/ill-fated'). خستگان را is marked by را and functions as the affected party 'the weary (people)'; امید is the predicate noun 'hope' (the sentence’s main predication: '[this] was the hope of the weary'). The order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: subject phrase → affected party (را) → predicate noun.
segments.s292-g45: رسیده is a past participle/predicate meaning 'arrived' or 'had come'. به میراث marks the goal ('to the inheritance'); نزدیک شاه qualifies میراث as 'near the king' or 'the royal/king-adjacent inheritance' (نزدیک used adjectivally/relationally). The phrase describes his attainment of that inheritance.
segments.s292-g46: به بازوش = 'on/with his arm' (بازو + possessive ش). بر + داشتی is a poetic verbal construction meaning 'he bore/held up upon'; داشتی here is the past sense 'had/held'. سال و ماه ('years and months') are the objects—figuratively 'years and months' = the time/duration he carried (i.e., he bore the years of rule).
segments.s292-g47: چو = 'when'. مهر دلش ('the love of his heart') is the noun phrase acting as the experiencer/agent of the volition. گستهم را is the person/object desired (marked by را). بخواست is past tense 'he desired/longed for' (or 'willed'); the clause means 'when his heart's love desired Gostaham'.
segments.s292-g48: گشاد is used as a past action 'opened/loosed' (here transitive). آن گرانمایه = 'that precious (thing/jewel/amulet)' is the direct object. از دست راست = 'from (his) right hand' (از = from; دست راست = right hand). The sequence describes him taking/loosening the precious item from his right hand.
segments.s292-g57: دو هفته (numeral + noun) is the temporal subject 'two weeks'. برآمد is a past-tense verb here meaning 'came/occurred' or idiomatically 'passed' in time expressions; the preposition بر with آمد (بر آمد) gives the sense 'came upon / passed over'. آن خسته مرد is a determiner + adjective + noun 'that weary man' (آن before the adjective); the whole phrase means 'two weeks passed over that weary man.'
segments.s292-g58: سر آمد is a verbal phrase meaning 'came to an end' / 'was finished' (سر + آمد used idiomatically). همه modifies the following list as 'all'. رنج و سختی و درد are a triad of synonyms ('suffering, hardship, and pain') joined by و; the clause therefore states that all suffering and hardship ended.
segments.s292-g59: بر اسبش is a prepositional phrase 'onto his horse' (اسب + enclitic ـش = 'his horse'). ببردند is past tense, 3rd-person plural ('they carried/brought'); its placement after the prepositional phrase is normal in classical word order. نزدیک شاه 'near the king' is the final locative phrase.
segments.s292-g60: چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause. شاه اندر او کرد لختی نگاه is a light-verb construction: نگاه (a noun 'glance') takes کردن (کرد) to form 'to cast a glance'; اندر او functions as 'at him/in him' (here 'at him'). لختی is an adverbial 'for a short while' modifying the action; overall: 'when the king cast a brief glance at him.'
segments.s292-g61: به ایرانیان is the dative phrase 'to the Iranians'. گفت is the verb 'said' (here 3rd singular); کز is the poetic contraction of 'که/که از' introducing a subordinate clause ('that/from'). کردگار is 'the Creator/God' and begins the content of what was said. The clause is thus: 'he said to the Iranians that (from) the Creator ...'.
segments.s292-g62: بود is the past copula 'was/there was' (here marking a past-tense predication). هر کسی is 'every/each person' (subject). شاد is the predicate adjective 'happy'. و links to the following phrase; به روزگار is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in (their) fortune/by fate' — together: 'each person was happy and blessed by fate.'
segments.s292-g63: ولیکن is a formal conjunction 'but/yet'. شگفت is an adjective 'astonishing/strange'. است is the copula 'is'. این کار من is a noun phrase 'this deed/act of mine' (این = 'this', کار = 'deed', من = 'my'). The clause means 'But this deed of mine is strange.'
segments.s292-g64: بدین راستی is an adverbial phrase 'by/with this truth' (بدین = به + این, 'by this'; راستی = 'truth' / 'indeed'). بر شده is a compound meaning 'has befallen / has come upon' (preposition بر + شده 'happened/been done'), indicating something happened to someone. یار من 'my companion' is the experiencer. The whole means 'in this truth my companion has thus been struck/have suffered.'
segments.s292-g65: The phrase places the cause/subject before the verb in a poetic order: به پیروزی (about/over victory) modifies the following locative clause. اندر غم گستهم = 'I have wandered in sorrow'; گستهم is the archaic/poetic past first‑person form of گشتن ('I wandered/turned'). Together the segment means 'I wandered into/was in sorrow over victory.'
segments.s292-g66: نکرد is the negated past of کردن ('did not make/caused not to be') and takes the definite object marked by را. این دل شادمان را is 'this joyful heart' as the direct object, and دژم is the predicative adjective 'sullen'; literal order is 'did not make this joyful heart sullen.'
segments.s292-g67: بخواند is a poetic/past form meaning 'he called/summoned' (here: 'he summoned'). آن زمان is an adverbial time phrase 'at that time' (آن = 'then/that'), and the names بیژن گیو with the object marker را form the direct objects: 'he summoned Bijan and Giv.'
segments.s292-g68: بدو is the dative pronoun 'to him/unto him'; داد is the simple past 'gave'; دست is the direct object 'hand' (here idiomatically 'help/assistance' or a literal object). گو نیو را is a noun phrase following دست with the object marker را; poetically the line says 'he gave him the hand, the ney' (i.e. he gave him the reed/ney or gave aid described by 'ney'). Word order is somewhat compressed and poetic.
segments.s292-g69: که introduces an explanatory/reason clause here ('for'/'because'); تو is the subject 'you'. The predicates نیک‌بختی and یزدان‌شناس are nominative/nominal predicates without an explicit copula (a common poetic omission) meaning 'you are fortunate and pious/God‑knowing.'
segments.s292-g70: This clause gives the admonition/result: هرگز هراس = 'never (have) fear.' مدار here is functioning as an imperative/negative command meaning roughly 'do not (be)'; از تن خویش = 'for your body/for yourself' (خویش is the possessive element attached to خوی + ش). The literal order is imperative + prepositional phrase + negative adverb + noun: 'Do not ever fear for your body.'
segments.s292-g71: همه is the subject 'all/everything'; مهر پروردگار is a genitive phrase 'the Lord's kindness/love' and است is the copula 'is'. و بس is an idiom meaning 'and that is enough' or 'and nothing more' emphasizing exclusivity.
segments.s292-g72: ندانم is the first‑person negative form 'I do not know' (here 'I know not'). به گیتی is the locative phrase 'in the world'; جز او هیچ‌کس = 'no one except him' (جز = 'except', او = 'him', هیچ‌کس = 'no one'). The whole line: 'I know no one in the world besides Him.'
segments.s292-g49: ابر here denotes a compress/sponge used as a dressing and comes before the noun phrase. بازوی گستهم is a possessive noun phrase 'the arm of Gostaham.' بر ببست is the past tense verb phrase: the preposition بر + the archaic past form ببست (3rd singular of بستن with the poetic prefix ب-) meaning 'bound (onto)'. The order places the instrument (ابر) before the object and then the verb phrase 'bound on Gostaham's arm.'
segments.s292-g50: بمالید is the past 3rd-singular of مالیدن with the poetic b- prefix, meaning 'he/they rubbed.' بر introduces the target of the rubbing ('on/upon'). خستگیهاش is خستگی + plural/collective marker + possessive -اش 'his/its weariness/fatigues.' دست ('hand') is the concrete instrument placed after the object for poetic emphasis: 'they rubbed (with) the hand on his weariness.'
segments.s292-g51: پزشکان is the plural noun 'physicians.' که is a relative pronoun 'who/that' linking this noun to the following origin clause. از روم و ز هند و چین is a coordinate list of origins: از and the poetic ز both mean 'from,' with و joining the items (Rome, India, China). Word order is noun — relative clause — list of origins.
segments.s292-g52: چه here functions as an enumerative/coordinating particle ('and also' / 'as well'), introducing a further source group. از شهر یونان و ایران‌زمین: از 'from' + شهر 'cities' + یونان 'Greece' coordinated with و و 'and' then ایران‌زمین 'the Iranian realm.' The phrase lists additional regions/cities of origin.
segments.s292-g53: به بالین indicates direction/place 'to the bedside' (به + بالین). گستهمشان is the proper name گستهم with the 3rd‑person plural possessive/associative suffix -شان; in context this marks the bedside as belonging to/associated with Gostaham and yields 'at Gostaham’s bedside.' بر نشاند is the past verb (بر + نشاند, archaic past of نشاندن) meaning 'sat (down)' or 'set (down),' where the prefix ب-/بر is the classical verbal prefix common in epic style.
segments.s292-g54: ز هر گونه افسون: ز 'of/from' + هر گونه 'every kind of' + افسون 'charm/enchantment.' بر او is the prepositional phrase 'over/on him.' The verb بر بخواند (بر + بخواند) is an archaic/prefixal past form meaning 'they recited' or 'they chanted'—the initial بر is the verbal prefix and بخرانده signals recitation. Overall: 'from every kind of charm they recited over him.'
segments.s292-g55: وز آنجا: وز is a poetic conjunction/particle introducing a change of place ('and/from there'); آنجا 'there.' بیامد is past 3rd-singular 'he came.' به جای نماز is a locative phrase 'to the place of prayer' (به + جای + نماز). The clause describes movement from the previous location to the prayer-place.
segments.s292-g56: بسی is an adverbial/quantifying word meaning 'much' / 'many' (used here to qualify what was spoken). با 'with' introduces the interlocutor or addressee; جهان‌آفرین is a title 'the Creator' (a compound noun). گفت is the simple past 'he said/spoke,' and راز 'secret' is the object—'he spoke many secrets to/with the Creator.' Word order in classical Persian places the object after the verb in such phrases.
segments.s292-g73: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause ('for/that'); اوی is an archaic emphatic third‑person pronoun 'he' placed before the copula; است is the present copular verb 'is'; جاوید is an adjective 'eternal' modifying the following noun; فریادرس is the predicate noun 'rescuer/one who brings succour'. Together: 'که اوی است جاوید فریادرس' = 'for he is the eternal rescuer.'
segments.s292-g74: به سختی is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in/under hardship' (به functioning adverbially here); نگیرد is third‑person singular negative present/subjunctive of گرفت/گیر 'does not/shall not take or seize' used here in a general sense 'does not grasp/does not give'; جز او is the exception phrase 'except his'; دست کس is the object phrase 'the hand of anyone' (here idiomatic: 'help from anyone'). Word order places the adverbial before the verb and the negated verb governs the object phrase.
segments.s292-g75: اگر introduces a conditional protasis 'if'; زنده is the predicate adjective 'alive'; گردد is the subjunctive/present form of گشت/گرد 'become' used here in the hypothetical clause ('should become'); تن مرده is the noun phrase 'dead body'; مرد appended names/specifies the person 'the man' (so: 'the dead body of the man'). The clause reads 'If the dead body of the man becomes alive...'.
segments.s292-g76: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign / lord of the world'; گستهم (a personal name here) is the direct object, marked by را; زنده + کرد is a light‑verb construction meaning 'made (someone) alive' — زنده is the adjective 'alive' and کرد (past of کردن) is the causative/past verb 'caused/made'. Word order is Subject – Object(را) – Predicate (past).
segments.s292-g77: بدآنگه is an adverb/adverbial phrase 'ill‑omened / inauspiciously / then (with bad omen)'; بدو is the prepositional object 'to him/unto him'; گفت is the past tense verb 'he said' (here used as the main saying verb); تیمار دار is an idiomatic exhortation 'take care / be mindful' formed from تیمار 'care' + دار (here functioning to produce a verbal/admonitory expression). The sequence means 'he said to him, "take care."'
segments.s292-g78: چو is the temporal/conditional conjunction 'when/as'; بیژن is the personal name 'Bijan' (subject); نبیند is the negative present/subjunctive of دیدن 'does not/shall not see' (here general/present negative 'does not see'); کس is the indefinite object 'anyone'; از روزگار is a prepositional phrase 'from/of fate' (here 'because of fate' or 'in the course of fate'). The clause reads 'when Bijan sees no one because of fate.'
segments.s292-g79: کز is a relative/causal particle 'that/from which/for which' introducing the clause; او 'him' indicates the cause/agent ('because of him' or 'from him'); رنج is the noun 'suffering/pain' as the object; بر here means 'for/on/for the sake of' and links to مهر 'love' so بر مهر = 'for love'; بگزیده‌ای is the perfective second‑person form 'you have chosen' (ب‑ prefix + گزیده + ای), so the clause says 'that you have chosen suffering for love of him.'
segments.s292-g80: ستایش is the noun 'praise' (the thing heard); بدین گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in this manner/way' (بدین = 'by this / in this', گونه = 'manner'); بشنیده‌ای is the perfective second‑person 'you have heard'. The construction is Object – Adverbial – Verb: 'You have heard praise in this way.'

sentences.s292-s41: As was fitting, the king also gave (for) a week dirhams and dinars and every kind of thing.
sentences.s292-s42: He sent messengers in every direction to the great and the free (nobles).
sentences.s292-s43: When you are freed from the war with the elders, now prepare a single battle for Kay Khosrow.
sentences.s292-s1: After that a cry rose from the lookout, for a band of horsemen appeared from the road.
sentences.s292-s2: I see from afar, with a single rider, three horses and two slain bound to him, pitiable.
sentences.s292-s3: All the renowned men of Iran mustered an army and, astonished, fixed their eyes on the road.
sentences.s292-s4: To see who it is from the borders of Turan who would dare to cross this plain of vengeance.
sentences.s292-s9: He kissed him and laid his face upon the ground; Khosrow became glad at the sight of him.
sentences.s292-s10: He asked and said to him, 'O lion-like man, where had you gone from the field of battle?'
sentences.s292-s11: Because of my wandering, Bijan recalled the matter (spoke); from eagerness and from the dust of Farshidvard.
sentences.s292-s12: And from that weariness and the lamentation of my wandering, from the warfare of the horsemen and from abundance and lack.
sentences.s292-s17: He was so ill from extreme weariness that, one might say, not even a breath came out of him.
sentences.s292-s18: Someone caught the scent of the king's affection, turned, and his gaze hurried toward him.
sentences.s292-s19: From his eyes fell tears of affection; the spahbed's face was made full of water and blood.
sentences.s292-s20: The great men were grief-stricken and wept over him, as if they had been seared on a fierce fire.
sentences.s292-s13: Now Gostaham has a single wish: that the task will not be difficult for the king.
sentences.s292-s14: A longing to see the king came upon him; and after that, if he were to die, it would be fitting.
sentences.s292-s15: Then the king commanded, “Seek Azram,” and they brought Gostaham before him.
sentences.s292-s16: The king became so kind‑hearted toward him that the weeping of his eyelashes bore fruit.
sentences.s292-s5: At the same time Bijan came forward, his garment's hem trailing; with his arm and the bow‑string he had loosed (cast) the bow.
sentences.s292-s6: Upon the horses — like (in the manner of) Lahāk and Farshidvard — he cast down the woeful, all covered with blood and dust.
sentences.s292-s7: On another horse — its back full of pain and sorrow — he went into the Turk's inner embrace.
sentences.s292-s8: When Bijan came near Khosrow, he saw his towering crown and throne.
sentences.s292-s21: The spahbed was filled with regret at the death, for the anvil of bitter hatred had crushed his wretched head beneath a rag.
sentences.s292-s22: From Hushang and Tahmuras and Jamshid, one bead — an ill token — became the hope of the weary.
sentences.s292-s23: He had come to the inheritance near the king; upon his arm he bore years and months.
sentences.s292-s24: When the love in his heart desired Gostaham, he loosened that precious thing from his right hand.
sentences.s292-s29: Two weeks passed over that weary man; all suffering, hardship, and pain came to an end.
sentences.s292-s30: They placed him on his horse near the king; when the king looked at him he cast a brief glance.
sentences.s292-s31: He told the Iranians that, from the Creator, each person was happy and in good fortune.
sentences.s292-s32: But this deed of mine is astonishing; by this truth my companion has thus been struck (has suffered).
sentences.s292-s33: I wandered into grief over victory, yet that did not make this joyful heart sullen.
sentences.s292-s34: At that time he summoned Bijan and Giv; to him he gave the hand (help) — the ney.
sentences.s292-s35: For you are fortunate and God‑knowing (pious); never fear for your own body.
sentences.s292-s36: All is the Lord's kindness and that's enough; I know no one in the world besides Him.
sentences.s292-s25: They bound a compress around Gostaham’s arm and rubbed his weariness with their hands.
sentences.s292-s26: Physicians who were from Rome and from India and China,
sentences.s292-s27: They sat down at Gostaham’s bedside and recited over him every kind of charm.
sentences.s292-s28: And from there he came to the place of prayer and there spoke many secrets with the Creator.
sentences.s292-s37: For he is the eternal rescuer; in hardship no one's hand will help except his.
sentences.s292-s38: If the dead man's body should come alive, the sovereign made Gostaham alive again.
sentences.s292-s39: Then, ill‑omened, he said to him, 'Take care — when Bijan sees no one because of fate.'
sentences.s292-s40: That you have chosen suffering for his love — you have heard praise told in this way.
